questions.question: Explain the five framework activities of a generic software process model with one example of a task for each.
keyPoints: Communication - involves requirements gathering and stakeholder interaction (e.g., conducting interviews)
Planning - defines project strategy and estimates (e.g., creating project schedule)
Modeling - creates representations of the system (e.g., designing use case diagrams)
Construction - builds the actual software (e.g., coding and unit testing)
Deployment - delivers and supports the software (e.g., installation and training)

questions.question: What are umbrella activities in a software process model? List any four umbrella activities with their purposes.
keyPoints: Umbrella activities run throughout the software process providing support and oversight
Project tracking - monitors progress against plans
Risk management - identifies and mitigates potential problems
Quality assurance - ensures products meet standards
Configuration management - controls changes to work products
Measurement - collects and analyzes process metrics

questions.question: Define the following terms: (i) Process Pattern (ii) Work Product (iii) Milestone (iv) Task Set
keyPoints: Process Pattern - template for solving recurring problems in software engineering
Work Product - tangible output produced as result of process activities
Milestone - point in schedule marking completion of specific task/deliverable
Task Set - collection of related tasks required to complete part of work

questions.question: List and explain any five key attributes of a software process.
keyPoints: Understandability - how clearly the process is defined and can be followed
Visibility - how clearly progress is visible to stakeholders
Robustness - how well the process handles unexpected problems
Maintainability - how easily the process can be adapted to changes
Speed - how quickly the process enables product delivery

questions.question: What is process tailoring? Why is it necessary in software engineering?
keyPoints: Process tailoring is adapting a process model to specific project needs
Necessity: Different projects have different requirements, team sizes, constraints
Allows flexibility in applying the process appropriately
Helps balance formality with practical needs
Enables organizations to optimize their processes for different project types

questions.question: Differentiate between a Task and a Work Product with suitable examples.
keyPoints: Task is a unit of work with clear outcome (e.g., conducting user interview, writing use case)
Work Product is tangible output produced (e.g., requirements document, design model)
Task transforms inputs to work products
Work products serve as inputs to subsequent tasks

questions.question: Explain the concept of a generic process model in detail. How does it serve as a foundation for developing specific software process models? Illustrate with suitable examples.
keyPoints: Generic process model is an abstract template/framework for software development
Provides structured approach with framework activities
Contains five core activities: Communication, Planning, Modeling, Construction, Deployment
Includes umbrella activities that provide support throughout
Serves as foundation by allowing customization through process tailoring
Specific models (Waterfall, Agile, etc.) are derived by adapting the generic model
Examples of adaptation: Waterfall makes it linear, Agile makes it iterative
Key benefit: Provides common vocabulary and structure for diverse approaches

questions.question: Describe the three general categories of software process models. Discuss the characteristics, advantages, and limitations of each category with examples.
keyPoints: Prescriptive/Linear models: Waterfall, V-model - sequential, document-driven (adv: structure, lim: inflexible)
Agile models: Scrum, XP - iterative, adaptive (adv: flexibility, lim: less predictable)
Hybrid models: Rational Unified Process - combines elements (adv: balanced, lim: complex)
Each category suits different project types and organizational needs
Prescriptive: Good for well-defined requirements
Agile: Good for rapidly changing requirements
Hybrid: Good for complex projects needing both structure and flexibility

questions.question: Explain the role of iteration in software process models. How does iteration help in managing risks and improving product quality? Give examples of how iteration is implemented in different process models.
keyPoints: Iteration allows repetition of activities with feedback and refinement
Helps manage risks by identifying problems early before they escalate
Improves quality through continuous review and improvement cycles
Waterfall: Limited iteration between phases (one pass)
Agile: Multiple short iterations (sprints in Scrum)
Spiral model: Iterations involve planning, risk analysis, engineering, evaluation
Each iteration produces an increment of working software
Iteration supports changing requirements and learning from feedback

questions.question: Discuss how umbrella activities contribute to the success of a software project. Select any two umbrella activities and explain their detailed implementation in a software project.
keyPoints: Umbrella activities provide cross-cutting support throughout development
Contribute to success by ensuring quality, managing risks, tracking progress
Configuration Management: Controls changes through change control board, version control systems, baselines
Risk Management: Risk identification workshops, risk assessment matrices, mitigation plans
Quality Assurance: Process audits, reviews, testing coordination
Project Tracking: Status meetings, progress reports, earned value analysis
Without umbrella activities, projects lack proper oversight and control
